content.full_text:
MEMORANDUM

TO:        Charles R. Mack, County Counsel

FROM:      Victoria E. Davis, Deputy County Counsel

DATE:      March 19, 1990

RE:        Conflict of Interest: Anne Scheuring


BACKGROUND:

Cache Creek Aggregates has applied for a use permit for a proposed
sand and gravel processing plant to be located near Capay.  The
application is pending before the Planning Commission.  Anne
Scheuring is a member of the Planning Commission.  She and her
husband own real property near the proposed project site.  The
Scheurings' property is currently used as a walnut orchard.  There
is a house on the property which is rented out to a tenant for its
fair market value of $100.00 per month.  The proposed project will
process an existing mound of aggregate.  A major side effect of the
project is increased truck traffic on the county road which borders
Mrs. Scheuring's property.  According to the Planning Commission's
staff report, the project will generate approximately 130 round
trips per day of gravel truck transport on a five day per week
basis.


QUESTION PRESENTED:

Does Mrs. Scheuring have a conflict of interest which prohibits her

Charles R. Mack
March 19, 1990
Page - 2

participation in the Planning Commission's decision regarding the

Cache Creek Aggregate use permit?


CONCLUSION:


Our conclusion is in the negative.


DISCUSSION:

A.     Government Code §§87100 et seq. set forth the broad ground

rules.

     Government Code §87100 (all citations are to the Government

Code unless otherwise specified), part of the Political Reform Act,

dictates that no state or local government public official shall

make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use her

official position to influence a governmental decision in which she

knows or has reason to know she has a financial interest.  A

"public official" is any member, officer, employee, or consultant

of a state or local government agency (excluding judges and court

commissioners).   Section 82048.  Pursuant to the parts of §87103

relevant to this discussion, an official has a financial interest

in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision

will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its

effect on the public generally, on any real property in which the

Charles R. Mack
March 19, 1990
Page - 3

official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or more.


B.  2 California Code of Regulations §§18702 et seq. set forth

regulations refining the broad rules.

     1.  2 CCR §18702 outlines the analysis to be applied:

          (a)  is the official's economic interest directly

involved?

          (b)  is the effect of the decision material?

          (c)  if the interest is not directly involved or the

effect is not material under 2 CCR §18702.1, determine whether the

effect is material under the appropriate regulation of 2 CCR

§§18702.2 through 18702.6.

          (d)  is the effect on the public official's interest

indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally?


     2.  Section 18702.1.  The effect of a decision regarding a

real property interest is material if the real property is the

subject of the decision (e.g., rezoning or annexation of the

official's property).

Charles R. Mack
March 19, 1990
Page - 4

3.     The pertinent part of 2 CCR §18702.3(a) provides that the

effect is material as to real property in which the official has

an ownership interest if the property is located outside the 300

foot radius but within a 2500 foot radius of the boundaries of the

property which is the subject of the decision and the decision will

have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of (A) $10,000 or

more on the fair market value of the official's real property; or

(B) will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more

per 12-month period.  2 CCR §18702.3(a)(3).


     Factors which shall be considered in determining whether the

decision will have the above effects include, but are not limited

to:


          (1)  proximity of the property which is the subject of

the decision and the magnitude of the proposed project or change

in use in relationship to the official's property;


          (2)  whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the

decision will affect the development potential or income producing

potential of the property;


          (3)  in addition to the foregoing, in the case of

residential property, whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the

decision will result in a change to the character of the

Charles R. Mack
March 19, 1990
Page - 5

neighborhood, including, but not limited to, effect on traffic,

view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or

similar traits of the neighborhood. 2 CCR §18720.3(d).


II. Application of the Law to the Facts at Hand.

A. The official in question has an ownership interest of more

than $1,000 in real property near the project site. According to

the statutes cited above, Mrs. Scheuring is a public official.

The question then becomes whether the decision to be made by the

Planning Commission will have a "material financial effect" on Mrs.

Scheuring's property. To determine what is a "material financial

effect," we turn to the regulations promulgated pursuant to the

Political Reform Act.


B. Application of Regulations.

Applying the analysis set forth in 2 CCR §§18702 et seq., we

can see that Mrs. Scheuring's property is not directly involved in

the decision to be made, because her property is not the subject

of the decision. 2 CCR 18702.1. Since 2 CCR §18702.1 does not

apply, we proceed to 2 CCR §18702.3, which sets forth criteria for

determining whether the effect of the decision is material. (See

above.) Mrs. Scheuring's property, according to the Assessor's

maps, is more than 300 feet but less than 2500 feet from the

Charles R. Mack
March 19, 1990
Page - 6

subject property.  Thus the effect of the decision will be
considered material if the decision will have a materially
foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market
value of Mrs. Scheuring's property or will affect the rental value
of the property by $1,000 or more over a 12 month period.  2 CCR
§18702.3(a)(3).  Since the current rental income from the property
is $100.00 per month, the rental value would have to plummet to
about $17.00 per month, which seems highly improbable.  The
question then becomes whether the Planning Commission's decision
could have an effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market value
of Mrs. Scheuring's property.


2 CCR §18702.3(d) sets forth factors to be considered in
determining such effects.  (See above.)  While the subject property
is fairly close in proximity to Mrs. Scheuring's property, the EIR
suggests that the magnitude of the proposed project is not great
enough to affect the current use of Mrs. Scheuring's property.
Mrs. Scheuring's property is already fully developed as a walnut
orchard, so the proposed project would appear not to hinder the
development potential.  The Scheuring property is zoned for
agricultural use.  The EIR indicates that the project will not
affect the walnut orchard's income production.


For the foregoing reasons, Mrs. Scheuring appears to have no
conflict of interest which would prohibit her participation in the

Charles R. Mack
March 19, 1990
Page - 7

Cache Creek Aggregate use permit decision.


VED/bp

March 19, 1990

neighborhood, including, but not limited to, effect on traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood. 2 CCR §18720.3(d).

To determine what is a "material financial effect," we turn to the regulations promulgated pursuant to the Political Reform Act.

Mrs. Scheuring's property, according to the Assessor's maps, is more than 300 feet but less than 2500 feet from the

Charles R. Mack  
March 19, 1990  
Page - 6

subject property. Thus the effect of the decision will be considered material if the decision will have a materially foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the fair market value of Mrs. Scheuring's property or will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more over a 12 month period. 2 CCR §18702.3(a)(3). The Scheuring property is zoned for agricultural use. The EIR indicates that the project will not affect the walnut orchard's income production.

For the foregoing reasons, Mrs. Scheuring appears to have no conflict of interest which would prohibit her participation in the

Charles R. Mack  
March 19, 1990  
Page - 7

Cache Creek Aggregate use permit decision.
